literal: the possessor-of-bhaga; bhaga = fortune, glory, opulence, lordliness; one who possesses the six excellences (aiśvarya, vīrya, yaśas, śrī, jñāna, vairāgya)
invariant_definition: *Brahman* under the aspect of personal-relational fullness — possessed of the six *bhagas* (sovereignty, valor, glory, prosperity, knowledge, dispassion). The classical definition is given at *Viṣṇu-Purāṇa* 6.5.74 (*aiśvaryasya samagrasya...*). All schools that develop devotional theology accept *Bhagavān* as a name of the highest reality under personal aspect; the schools differ on whether *Bhagavān* is the highest description (Vaiṣṇava: yes — *kṛṣṇas tu bhagavān svayam*, *Bhāgavata-Purāṇa* 1.3.28) or a *saguṇa* register (Advaita).

per_school.definition: *Bhagavān* is *Īśvara* under the aspect of personal devotional approach; identified with *saguṇa brahman*. Real for *upāsana* and for the devotional *jñāni*'s mature *bhakti* (Madhusūdana's *Bhakti-Rasāyana*); sublated in *nirguṇa brahman*-realization.

*Bhagavān* is the highest description of the highest reality — Kṛṣṇa as *svayaṃ-bhagavān* (literally 'Bhagavān-himself,' i.e., the source not derivative). The *Bhāgavata*-citation *kṛṣṇas tu bhagavān svayam* (1.3.28) is constitutive. *Brahman* and *Paramātman* are subordinate aspects of *Bhagavān*.
*Bhagavān* is a name of Viṣṇu (the highest reality) that emphasizes his fullness of qualities. Functionally equivalent to *Īśvara* in technical contexts.

translator_note: Translating *Bhagavān* as 'God' or 'the Lord' loses the technical content: *Bhagavān* is specifically the possessor of the six *bhagas* and is therefore an ontologically loaded title, not a generic divinity-term. In bhāṣya-prose the titles *Īśvara*, *Bhagavān*, *Paramātman*, and *Brahman* mean substantively different things and should not be conflated.
